summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – CHILDREN – Parenting Order – Where the majority of issues have been settled by consent – Where the remaining issues requiring determination are narrow –Where it is determined the father is not a flight risk – Where a Family Law Watchlist order will not be made – Where the father will not be required to pay a security bond – Where a limit on overseas travel will not be imposed – Where there will not be a restriction on holidaying in or transiting through non-Hague Convention countries – Where once the child turns 12 she will be able to be accompanied overseas with the persons nominated by the father – Where an order is made

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAREW J:

Ms Arundell (“the mother”) and Mr Skewes (“the father”) have one child together, namely, X, who was born on [redacted] 2018.
A final parenting order was made by consent on 1 October 2021 (“the 2021 order”). Both parties seek to discharge that order and replace it with a new parenting order, part of which restates provisions in the 2021 order, and part of which contains new or varied provisions. Most of the new or varied provisions are consented to by the parties but there remain a few issues that require determination.
On 20 December 2024, Gill J determined, as a preliminary matter pursuant to s 65DAAA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”), that the matters then in dispute were “matter[s] of sufficient importance to warrant the reconsideration of the proceedings as a whole”. This was in circumstances where the father was, at that time, seeking to relocate to Country B with the child and the mother was seeking to restrain the father from travelling internationally with the child.
The circumstances have moved on since then. The father no longer seeks to relocate and as this was the only real basis upon which the matter was proceeding in this Court, a request was made by Gill J on 16 December 2025, for the matter to be transferred to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2). Judges of this Court do not have the power to transfer matters to the lower court. For reasons that are entirely unclear, the matter was not transferred.
Given the narrow scope of the remaining issues, the matter proceeded, with the consent of the parties, via video link between Brisbane and City H.

ISSUES

The parties agreed that the only issues upon which a determination is now required are as follows:
(1) In relation to overseas travel:
(a) Whether the child’s name should be placed on the Family Law Watchlist preventing the child’s removal from Australia save for travel pursuant to the final order;

(b) Whether the father should pay a security bond if he travels overseas with the child and, if so, the quantum thereof;

(c) Which parent should hold the child’s Australian, United Kingdom (“UK”) and Country B passports, it being agreed that the mother will hold the child’s Country G passport;

(d) Whether a parent should be able to travel internationally with the child on more than one occasion each year and whether travel should be restricted to countries that are signatories to the Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction signed on 25 October 1980 (“the Hague Convention”) even if only in transit; and

(e) Whether, once the child turns 12 (and other than travelling with her school), she should be able to travel overseas in the company of nominated persons other than a parent;

(2) Whether the child should be provided an electronic device that also connects to the internet, or a ‘dumb phone’, in order to communicate with the parents as she wishes, and whether there should be specific communication provisions made for the child’s communication with each parent; and

(3) The form of any order relating to overseas registration of the parenting order.

Exhibit 10 sets out the comparative orders sought by each party on the above issues.

BACKGROUND

The mother was born on [redacted] 1982 and is currently 43 years old. She is an artist. The mother is a Country G citizen and was granted an Australian permanent residence visa on 22 January 2024. She has not re-partnered and has no other children.
The father was born on [redacted] 1972 and is currently 53 years old. He is a property developer. He is an Australian and Country B citizen and has a permanent residence visa in the UK. He has recently re-partnered with Ms R who lives in Melbourne.
The parent’s relationship commenced in March 2016, and they separated in March 2020.
Since October 2023, a 5:2:2:5 care arrangement has been in place for the child during school terms and school holiday time has been shared in accordance with the 2021 order.
The mother’s estimated legal costs up until the end of the one-day trial are $170,820.46 which has been funded by loans from friends.
The father’s estimated legal costs up until the end of the one-day trial are $661,407.80 which has been funded from his own funds and loans.

APPLICABLE PRINCIPLES

All parenting proceedings are regulated by Pt VII of the Act. In addition, s 43 of the Act requires the Court to have regard to several matters including:
(a) The need to protect the rights of children and to promote their welfare; and

(b) The need to ensure protection from family violence.

Every parenting decision requires the application of the relevant parts of Part VII of the Act which sets out the objects and matters that must be considered when determining what parenting order is proper.[1] What parts are relevant will depend to a large degree on the issues in dispute. As earlier noted, the issues in dispute in this case are very narrow.
In deciding whether to make a particular parenting order, the Court must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration (s 60CA of the Act).
The best interests of the child are determined by reference to the matters set out in s 60CC of the Act and include: arrangements that promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect or other harm) of the child and each person who has care of the child; any views expressed by the child; the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child; the capacity of each person who has or will have parental responsibility to provide for the child’s needs; the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so; and in considering those matters, the Court must consider any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child, and any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.
Although I may not specifically discuss in these reasons each subparagraph of each relevant section of the Act, I have considered all sections as required when making my determination.[2]
Turning then to consider the issues.

OVERSEAS TRAVEL

Whether the child’s name should be placed on the Family Law Watchlist preventing the child’s removal from Australia save for travel pursuant to the final order; and

Whether the father should pay a security bond if he travels overseas with the child and, if so, the quantum thereof.

It seems to me that the issues about the Family Law Watchlist order and the security bond are conveniently considered together.
The mother initially sought the “continuation” of a Family Law Watchlist order.
There was some confusion about whether a Family Law Watchlist order is currently in place. Neither party had a copy of the order, and I was informed by them that no such order was accessible on the Commonwealth Courts portal. I was further informed that both parties had recently engaged new lawyers.
On the Courts’ file (Courtpath) it appears that an order to place the child’s name on the Family Law Watchlist may have been made by a Judge of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australian (Division 2) on 22 June 2023 on an ex parte basis, after hours, and “[u]ntil further order”. A notation to the order states that the mother “appeared by telephone in person”. Although sealed and signed by a registrar, the order appears to be in draft form, in that, while there is provision for the names of the parties and their dates of birth, that part of the order is incomplete. The order was not published. Further, although the order states that it was made “upon application” there was no application filed at that time, nor on the face of the order, was leave granted to make an oral application. The mother did not commence proceedings until 26 June 2023.
It does not appear that either party made enquiries with the Australian Federal Police to ascertain whether the child’s name is on the Family Law Watchlist. To do so is a simple procedure requiring the submission of a form and the provision of identification. In any event, both parties have proceeded on the basis that such an order was made, and the father seeks an order for its removal. Why that would be necessary is unclear having regard to the effect of r 5.01 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) which provides as follows:
On the making of final orders in a proceeding, any interlocutory order made in the proceeding pending further order is automatically discharged and ceases to have continuing effect.
Without a fresh order that the child’s name be placed on the Family Law Watchlist, the earlier order, if made, will lapse upon the making of the final parenting order.
Leave was granted, without objection, for the mother to further amend the orders sought by her to seek an order for the child’s name to be placed on the Family Law Watchlist until she turns 16 save for travel pursuant to the final order.
If a Family Law Watchlist order is made, the mother proposes that the Family Law Watchlist be “suspended” when either parent is travelling overseas with the child if pursuant to the order.
The mother contends that the father is a flight risk and if permitted to travel overseas with the child he may not return her to Australia. However, the mother does consent to the father travelling overseas with the child on one occasion each year but only to a Hague Convention country (even if in transit). There is an obvious inconsistency with the mother’s position. Either the father is a flight risk or he is not.
The mother’s objection to the father’s travel with the child appears to be more an objection to him travelling overseas with the child more than once per year. The parents have agreed that the child will spend the whole of each mid-year school holiday with one or other of the parents (so that in even numbered years the child will spend all of the Term 1 holidays (two weeks) and the Term 3 holidays (two weeks) with the mother and the child will spend all of the Term 2 holidays (three weeks) with the father) alternating each year. Conceivably, the father might travel overseas with the child on three occasions in a year in which the child spent the Term 1 and Term 3 and part of the Term 4 holidays with him. In the following year, his time with the child during holidays would only be the Term 2 and part of the Term 4 holidays. The mother’s objection was that she did not want the child travelling overseas “every holiday” because she might be jetlagged. I am not persuaded that the mother’s objection to the extent of possible overseas travel warrants the imposition of a Family Law Watchlist order.
The mother submits that a Family Law Watchlist order would afford her some comfort and minimise her anxiety. These matters are also relied upon to support her submission that the father should lodge a security bond of $500,000 prior to travelling overseas with the child.
The source of the mother’s anxiety is contended to arise because of the father’s conduct in June/July 2023 when he withheld the child from her for six weeks. This was the catalyst for the mother recommencing the parenting proceedings and the mother contends that she feared the father would relocate to Country B with the child. The father returned the child to the mother in the week before the first mention date on 8 August 2023.
The withholding of the child for six weeks, while unfortunate in the scheme of things, was not entirely of the father’s own making.
Correspondence sent on behalf of the father on 23 June 2023, outlined the statements made by the child to the father over the period 27 February 2023 to 21 June 2023 suggesting, among other things, that the mother had thrown objects at the child when angry, had dragged the child, and yelled at her that she was stupid. The letter readily acknowledged that there may be other explanations for the child’s comments and suggested that the parties attend upon a psychologist (who was named in the 2021 order) on 27 June 2023, to discuss the matter and find a way forward. The father proposed that the child communicate with the mother via video calls in the meantime. Video calls did occur on an almost daily basis for one to two hours.
The mother commenced proceedings on 26 June 2023.
In further correspondence sent to the mother on 27 June 2023, it was noted that she had cancelled the appointment with the psychologist. The father offered to attend a new appointment on short notice. The mother’s lawyers responded to the concerns raised by the father, in a letter dated 3 July 2023 (although the letter itself is not before me). In a letter dated 25 July 2023, the father’s lawyers proposed the parties attend an urgent mediation. In a further letter dated 27 July 2023, the father expressed surprise that the mother stated in her affidavit that she feared the father would take the child to Country B without the mother’s consent. He assured the mother that he would not take the child to Country B without the mother’s consent or an order of the court. The father again raised the prospect of mediation.
On 4 August 2023, the equal time arrangement resumed.
The mother submits that the father’s conduct significantly damaged her ability to trust him.
While that may be the case, there does not appear to have been any further interruption to what appears to have been a largely cooperative coparenting relationship. Each parent spent extra time with the child while the other parent was either overseas or interstate on occasions in 2023, 2024, and 2025.
The mother contends that her anxiety about the father not returning the child, relates not only to the withholding of the child in 2023, but his alleged attitude to her role as a mother including that he has been controlling during negotiations.
Some examples of the father’s attitude to the mother and his controlling behaviour are provided by the mother as follows:
(a) The father excluded her from the child’s baptism ceremony in 2022;

(b) The father’s insistence that the child be cared for by his parents or the child’s nanny when he was unavailable and the mother was otherwise available;

(c) The father’s accusations in 2022 that the mother was causing the child to become upset at changeovers and that he said to her in front of the child that she was evil;

(d) In 2023, when the child was resistant to returning to the mother’s care, the father alleged that it was because the mother was being abusive; and

(e) The father’s insistence that they should all move to Country B.

The communications which form part of Exhibit 8, do not support the mother’s contention that the father insisted that they all move to Country B and in so doing was controlling of the mother. To the contrary, in a series of text messages to the father on 20 July 2022, the mother indicated her wish to relocate to Country B, even stating at one point, the “[s]ooner the better”. The mother had never been to Australia prior to 2019 and had lived in various places around the world prior to that. The parties came to Australia at the end of 2019, initially for a holiday but, at least initially, their departure was curtailed by the COVID pandemic.
The father travelled to Country B with the child between 24 June 2022 and 25 July 2022 before returning to Australia. There is no suggestion he did so without the mother’s consent.
In March 2023, the child was enrolled in a private school in City J, Country B, initially with the parties’ consent.
The father travelled to Country B with the child on 17 April 2023, and the child attended an interview at the school in City J on 25 April 2023. The mother joined the father and the child in Country B and the child was in the mother’s care from 26 to 30 April 2023 before returning to the father’s care from 1 to 9 May 2023. The parties then travelled separately to City S, Country T, where the child was again reunited with the mother and she and the mother returned to Australia on 12 May 2023 and the father returned later.
It seems that at some point the mother changed her mind about moving to Country B.
On 21 December 2023, the father wrote to the mother’s solicitor suggesting that they have a round table discussion at the mother’s lawyer’s office and suggested a Family Report be prepared. The mother’s solicitor replied that he would seek instructions and revert to the father in the new year.
The father followed up his 21 December 2023 communication to the mother’s solicitor on 11 March 2024, as follows:
I am following up on a note we exchanged on 21 December 2023 in respect to a round table discussion to be hosted by you. Achieving a resolution outside the courtroom would serve [the child’s] best interests. I would like to raise at the meeting the prospect of [the mother], [the child] & myself, relocating to [City J], given my work obligation. This is something that I have recently raised by email to [the mother].

I’d like to understand [the mother’s] perspective for a relocation to [City J].

I understand that [the mother’s] needs are crucial to this discussion, and I look forward to an open and transparent dialogue. I am committed to a mediated approach that prioritises [the child’s] interests and respects [the mother’s] needs as an artist and a mother. I propose to provide for both of them should we agree to relocate to [City J].
The father’s communications appear to be conciliatory and respectful. I am not persuaded on the evidence relied upon by the mother that the father was “controlling”.
Indeed, it is the father who capitulated and withdrew his application to relocate with the child to Country B. He accepts that the child needs both parents in her life and that the mother no longer wishes to move to Country B. The father has rearranged his business operations to accommodate the fact that he will now be remaining in Australia.
I do not regard the other matters raised by the mother relating to events or incidents in 2022 and 2023 as sufficient to justify the making of Family Law Watchlist order nor for the imposition of a security bond prior to travel with the child. Indeed, it is common ground that the father travelled overseas with the child in 2022 and in 2023 with the mother’s consent.
Both parties agreed that relevant principles to take into account when considering whether a security bond should be imposed are set out in Line & Line,[3] where the Full Court set out the following matters to be taken into account in assessing the risk posed by a parent of not returning to Australia:
(a) The existence (or otherwise) of continuing ties between the departing parent and Australia (such as the ownership of real estate, the existence of business interests, or the residence of close family or friends here);

(b) The existence and strength of possible motives not to return (including the level of conflict between the parents, particularly over child-related issues);

(c) The existence and strength of possible motives to remain in the other nominated country (such as the ownership of real estate, the existence of business interests, or the residence of close family and/or personal friends there);

(d) Whether the country to which the departing parent intends to travel is or is not a signatory to the Hague Convention; and

(e) The financial circumstances of the parties.[4]

The Full Court accepted that there was a two-fold purpose for requiring a security bond. Firstly, to provide an incentive to return and secondly, to provide adequate funds for the party left in Australia to take action to recover the child.[5]
The father has significant family connections in Australia. His parents have lived in City H since 1964. The father’s brother and his family live in City H, and his children attend the same school as the child. The father has a large extended family who live in City H and in other cities in Australia. The father operates a business in Australia while also maintaining oversight of the business in Country B but to a lesser extent than previously. His family connections in Country B are minimal. The head office for his company, of which he is one of four directors, is located in City H and it also has offices in Melbourne and Country B. The father has recently re-partnered with a woman who lives in Melbourne, Australia. The father has a 40-year association with the school attended by the child and is an active member of the U Church and the V Club in City H. The father has lived in Australia for significant periods of his life and when living overseas continued to regularly return to Australia to visit family and friends.
The father has lived in the UK and in Country B in the past and owns real property in both places as he does in Australia, although he has no equity in the Australian property. The mother contends that most of the father’s wealth (which she contends is in the vicinity of $10,000,000-$11,000,000) is located overseas and that this increases the risk he poses of not returning to Australia with the child. The father disputes that his net worth is as submitted by the mother but does not dispute that he is in a far stronger financial position than the mother and that most of his assets (which he has owned for a very long time) are in the UK or Country B.
As noted earlier, the mother submits that the quantum of any security bond should be set at $500,000. The mother provided no evidence to support the necessity for such a quantum. It was submitted that such a sum was reasonable as it represented about five per cent of the father’s net wealth. No authority was cited to support the proposition that the quantum of a security bond should be calculated in that way. I was not taken to any case in which such a large security bond was imposed. The father submits that if it is determined that a security bond should be imposed, a sum of $20,000 is more than adequate.
The countries with which the father has a close association other than Australia, namely, Country B and the UK, are both signatories to the Hague Convention which means that if the father wrongfully retains the child in one of those two jurisdictions (or any other country that is a signatory to the Hague Convention) the mother may make an application to the Central Authority in Australia to obtain the child’s return to Australia. The Central Authority in Australia does not charge for its services in assisting the return of a child wrongfully removed from Australia but the Central Authorities in overseas jurisdictions may do so. There is no evidence about that. If a return order were made it would be expected that the mother would be required to pay for airfares, possibly for herself to retrieve the child, and accommodation pending the child being placed in her care.
I am not persuaded by the arguments submitted on behalf of the mother, that the father should be required to lodge a security bond prior to travel overseas with the child, whether for the sum of $500,000 or any other sum. In my view, the father’s long-term connections with Australia are significant and his motives to return to Australia are significant. Even when not living in Australia, the father has regularly returned to Australia, which would make him amendable to sanctions and penalties under Australian law if he were to wrongfully retain the child in an overseas jurisdiction. The countries with which he has an overwhelming connection are both signatories to the Hague Convention. The father has travelled overseas with the child with the mother’s consent in 2022 and 2023 and returned. The father withdrew his application to relocate the child to Country B because of his acceptance of the importance of the mother and the father in the child’s life and that the mother does not wish to relocate.

Which parent is to hold the child’s Australian, UK and Country B passports, it being agreed that the mother will hold the child’s County G passport

Pursuant to the 2021 order, the father held all the child’s passports. That order was made by consent, and both parties were legally represented. Under the current proposed order to be made by consent, the mother will now hold the child’s Country G passport. I am not persuaded that she should hold all of the child’s passports.

Whether a parent should be able to travel internationally with the child on more than one occasion each year and whether travel should be restricted to countries that are signatories to the Hague Convention countries, even if only in transit

The parents have reached agreement that the child should be able to travel overseas with each of her parents. The mother wishes to restrict the opportunities for the child’s travel overseas with the father to one occasion each year and by holding all the child’s passports she can ensure that outcome.
As previously identified in these reasons, the mother raised a concern that the child might be travelling overseas with the father on every school holiday and that this may be arduous for the child. Even if that is correct, on any given year overseas travel would occur on a maximum of three times in the alternate year when the child spends the holidays after Terms 1, 3, and 4 with the father. If the mother also travelled with the child overseas it could conceivably be more than three times.
However, I was not taken to any evidence that either parent would be likely to act in a way that was contrary to the best interests of the child.
As I am not persuaded that the father poses a flight risk, I see no reason to restrict the father’s ability to holiday in or transit through a country that is not a signatory to the Hague Convention or to limit his overseas travel with the child to once a year.

Whether, once the child turns 12 (and other than travelling with her school), she should be able to travel overseas in the company of nominated persons other than a parent

It is not in dispute that the child has a close and loving relationship with the persons nominated by the father as appropriate persons to accompany the child overseas in the event she does not travel with him once she turns 12.
The mother’s objection to a person other than herself and the father accompanying the child overseas after she turns 12 is based on a concern that such persons may not return the child to Australia, and that she would be left without legal remedy. As submitted by Senior Counsel for the father, such persons would be amenable to prosecution under Australian law (see s 65Y of the Act) and the ability of the mother to proceed under the Hague Convention is not negated because a person other than the father wrongfully retains the child in an overseas jurisdiction.[6]
I am not persuaded to restrict the persons able to accompany the child overseas once she turns 12 to just the father and the mother.

WHETHER THE CHILD SHOULD BE PROVIDED AN ELECTRONIC DEVICE THAT ALSO CONNECTS TO THE INTERNET, OR A ‘DUMB PHONE’, IN ORDER TO COMMUNICATE WITH THE PARENTS AS SHE WISHES, AND WHETHER THERE SHOULD BE SPECIFIC COMMUNICATION PROVISIONS MADE FOR THE CHILD’S COMMUNICATION WITH EACH PARENT

The mother submits that the father should be required to provide a “dumb phone” to the child, which I was informed was a phone without internet access. The mother’s proposal is that in addition to the child being at liberty to contact the parent she is not then living with at all reasonable times, such contact should occur at a minimum on each alternate night at 6.00 pm. The form of order proposed by the mother would require the phone to travel with the child between homes and any costs associated with the phone should be borne by the father. If travelling overseas, the mother proposes that the child communicate with the other parent on the travelling parent’s phone.
The father submits that he should provide to the child “an age appropriate electronic device” to enable her to contact her parents at all reasonable times and if she has not done so for more than five consecutive nights, the parent with the care of the child should be required to facilitate a call with the other parent at 6.30 pm in the time zone in which the child is then spending time. The father proposes that each parent should be subjected to various mandatory injunctions to ensure that the device does not interfere with the child’s school etc and to ensure that the child does not have access to social media until she is 16. Lastly, he proposes that if the device is lost or damaged, the parent with whom the child is then living should pay the costs of replacement.
Each parent’s proposals invite dispute e.g. if the device is lost but neither parent knows when it was lost; if the order mandates communication each alternate night at 6.00 pm.
The child currently spends significant time with each of her parents and will continue to do so. In my view, whether the child is provided with a phone or some other device is a matter for each parent to decide. It might be that one parent wishes to provide the child with a phone or other device and the other does not but there seems to be no reason why the arrangement needs to be the same in each household.
More importantly, given her tender age, the responsibility for communicating with her parents should not be a matter that rests on the child’s shoulders. Communication should be facilitated between the child and the other parent but given the significant time she spends with each parent I am not prepared to mandate that communication occur any more than once in a five-day period.

THE FORM OF ANY ORDER RELATING TO OVERSEAS REGISTRATION OF THE PARENTING ORDER

The mother’s proposal mandates the parties taking steps to register the parenting order in Country B, Country G, and the UK prior to any overseas travel and at the father’s cost. Such an order would preclude any overseas travel until registration had occurred.
The father’s proposal only creates an obligation on the parties to register the parenting order in those countries once a request has been made by one parent and at that parent’s cost, although during submissions it was conceded on behalf of the father that he would pay any costs associated with registration.
I was not taken to any evidence that the parenting order can be registered in each of the nominated jurisdictions let alone the procedure and cost of doing so. No submissions were made as to the necessity of registering the order in foreign jurisdictions when the parents and the child live in Australia. I decline to make any order in relation to the overseas registration of the order.

DISPOSITION

The issues are determined as follows:
(1) In relation to overseas travel:
(a) There will be no Family Law Watchlist order made;

(b) The father will retain the child’s Australian, UK and Country B passports and the mother will retain the child’s Country G passport;

(c) The father will not be required to pay a security bond if he travels overseas with the child;

(d) There will be no requirement for a parent travelling overseas with the child to only travel to or through countries that are signatories to the Hague Convention; and

(e) Once the child turns 12 she will be able to be accompanied overseas with the persons nominated by the father;

(2) There will be no order for the child to be provided with a ‘dumb phone’ or an ‘electronic device’ and no order for the parent with whom the child is spending time with from time to time to facilitate communication between the child and the other parent other than once if the child is spending time with that parent for up to five nights or more;

(3) There will be no order requiring registration of the order in an overseas jurisdiction.
